findings.description: parse() builds an 'index' dict from the header (line 36) but never uses it — all fields are accessed by hard-coded positional indices (row[0]..row[3]); if the CSV columns appear in any order other than id/name/amount/email, wrong values are silently assigned to wrong fields or a ValueError is raised on the wrong column.

parse() accesses row[0] through row[3] with no guard on row length; a short row (e.g., a trailing blank line or a row with fewer than 4 fields) raises an uncaught IndexError, crashing the entire parse instead of skipping or reporting the bad row.

float(row[2]) will raise ValueError if an 'amount' cell contains a float-formatted integer string such as '1.0' passed after int() coercion elsewhere, or whitespace-padded values that survive CSV parsing; no per-row exception handling means a single malformed cell aborts ingest of all subsequent rows.

count_rows() opens the file without a 'with' statement, leaking the file handle on every call; on systems with low file-descriptor limits this will eventually cause 'Too many open files' failures in long-running pipelines.

count_rows() returns len(lines) - 1, so an empty file (zero lines) returns -1 — a nonsensical negative count that callers cannot distinguish from an error; the correct result for an empty file is 0 (or an explicit error).

count_rows() counts raw text lines via readlines(), not CSV records; a CSV with quoted multiline fields (RFC 4180 compliant) will report an inflated count equal to the number of newline characters, not the number of actual data rows.

export() uses os.path.basename(filename) for path safety, but if filename is an empty string, os.path.basename('') returns '' and os.path.join(export_dir, '') resolves to the directory path itself, causing open() to raise IsADirectoryError with no caller-visible explanation.

export() opens the destination file for writing before iterating records; if a write fails mid-loop the destination is left as a partially written, structurally invalid CSV file with no rollback or cleanup.

The 'index' variable built from the header on line 36 is assigned but never referenced anywhere in parse(); it is dead code that misleads readers into thinking column lookup is position-independent when it is not.
